Dense hedges: A narrow, continuous strip of densely planted shrubs or small trees forming a barrier that provides ecological connectivity, wildlife shelter, noise reduction, and wind protection.
Ecological corridors - Pollinator-friend: Areas of land or water that maintain or restore ecological connectivity, enabling species movement and natural processes between habitats.
Flower beds: Urban planted areas with ornamental flowering herbaceous species aimed at enhancing biodiversity, supporting pollinators, and providing aesthetic value.
Linear hedges: Continuous lines of closely spaced shrubs or small trees that form a low or medium-height green barrier, supporting local biodiversity and acting as landscape elements.
Row of trees: A linear arrangement of individual trees planted at regular intervals, mainly for shading, aesthetic enhancement, and microclimate regulation in urban areas.
Rows / tree lines: Linear formations of trees (with varying spacing) functioning as windbreaks, ecological corridors, and structural landscape elements, often at a larger scale.
Shading tree groups / tree clusters: Groups or clusters of trees designed to provide shade, reduce urban heat island effects, and create microhabitats within urban settings.
Shrub patches: Localized groups of shrubs that create shelter for fauna, contribute to noise mitigation, and enhance urban biodiversity.
Single tree: An isolated tree in an urban or peri-urban area providing shade, carbon sequestration, air quality improvement, and aesthetic value.
Depaving: Removal of impervious surfaces (e.g., asphalt, concrete) to restore soil permeability, reduce runoff, and enhance water infiltration.
Permeable paving: Paving systems designed to allow water to infiltrate through surfaces, reducing stormwater runoff and promoting groundwater recharge.
Artificial wetlands: Constructed wetland systems that mimic natural wetlands to treat stormwater, support biodiversity, and provide ecosystem services.
Bioretention areas: Vegetated depressions designed to collect and treat stormwater runoff through filtration, adsorption, and biological uptake.
Detention basins: Engineered basins that temporarily store stormwater to manage peak flows and reduce flood risks.
Filter trenches / strips / drains: Linear features filled with vegetation or porous media that filter and slow down stormwater runoff.
Floodable park: Urban green space designed to accommodate temporary flooding during heavy rainfall, reducing downstream flood risks.
Fountains: Artificial water features providing aesthetic value, microclimate cooling, and recreational benefits in urban spaces.
Ground-level fountain: A fountain integrated into pavement level, often interactive, providing cooling and play opportunities.
Green channels: Vegetated or semi-vegetated open channels designed for stormwater conveyance and treatment.
Mist sprayers: Devices that release fine water mist to cool microclimates and improve thermal comfort in public spaces.
Rain garden: Small vegetated basins that capture and infiltrate rainwater, filtering pollutants and supporting biodiversity.
Rain plaza: Urban square or plaza designed to temporarily hold and manage stormwater during intense rainfall events.
Reopened canals: Restored artificial canals that had been covered or filled, bringing back water flow, ecological functions, and aesthetic value.
Urban wetlands: Natural or constructed wetlands within urban areas that manage stormwater, improve water quality, and provide habitat.
Vegetated ditches: Shallow, vegetated channels designed for drainage and water quality improvement by slowing and filtering runoff.
Water blades: Thin, linear water features providing aesthetic and microclimatic functions.
Water mirror: Flat water surfaces designed for reflective and aesthetic effects in public spaces.
Climbing structures on walls or nets: Structures supporting climbing plants to provide vertical greening, shade, and microclimate benefits.
Extensive green roofs: Lightweight vegetated roofs (substrate ≤15 cm), with drought-tolerant plants, low maintenance, and stormwater management functions.
Green walls: Walls fully or partially covered with vegetation using modular or continuous systems for thermal insulation, biodiversity, and aesthetics.
Intensive green roofs: Vegetated roofs with deeper substrates (>15 cm), capable of supporting shrubs and trees, functioning as rooftop gardens.
Vegetal sunshades: Plant-based shading devices that reduce solar heat gain and improve energy performance.
Green integrated furnishings: Urban furniture elements (e.g., benches, planters) combined with vegetation for comfort and aesthetic value.
Green mobile covers: Movable shading structures incorporating vegetation to provide flexible shade and cooling.
Green rigid covers: Fixed structures integrating plants for permanent shading and microclimate regulation.
Green shelters: Shelters (e.g., bus stops) incorporating vegetation to provide shade, comfort, and aesthetic benefits.
Prefabricated green modules: Pre-assembled vegetated modules easily installed to add greenery to urban spaces.
Vegetated awnings: Plant-integrated canopies that provide shading and enhance microclimate comfort.
Community gardens: Collective urban gardens for growing food, fostering community cohesion, education, and biodiversity.
Green spaces in infrastructures (underpa: Vegetated areas integrated into grey infrastructure to transform underutilized spaces into ecological assets.
Micro parks: Small-scale urban parks offering recreational, aesthetic, and environmental services.
Multifunctional green spaces: Urban green areas designed to provide multiple ecosystem services (recreation, stormwater management, biodiversity).
Urban gardens: Urban areas allocated for food cultivation, education, and biodiversity support.
